data.title: MercadoLibre: Latin America's Amazon Is Still In The Early Innings
data.description:
Typically, when it comes to investing in growth stocks, we like to see three key company attributes before considering an investment.

First off, size and scale are key components of what we look for. We don’t have any hard rules for size requirements, given different industries are different sizes, but it’s a well-known business maxim that scale drives efficiency, crowds out competition, and tends to lead to better shareholder returns over the long term.

Secondly, we look for revenue growth in the mid-teens range or higher.

Strong revenue growth forms the backbone of any growth investment thesis, and it underscores a company's ability to monetize customers. To some degree, it also validates that demand exists for what a company is providing.

Finally, we also look for positive operating margins—or at least positive operating cash flow—to give us confidence that a business has been able to dial in its business model effectively. Growth is great, but unit economics also matter at the end of the day.

If company can’t achieve operating leverage and grow sales without linearly increasing costs, it’s going to be a difficult investment to make compounded returns on.

Out of the market today, there are many companies that fit this criteria, but one of our favorites is MercadoLibre (MELI), commonly known as the 'Amazon of Latin America'.

As the largest consumer marketplace company within Southern and Central America, MELI enjoys significant efficiencies of scale, a strong marketplace flywheel, and inherent customer lock-in.

Additionally, Mercado Pago—one of the largest consumer fintech companies in the region—further entrenches MELI's customers into its ecosystem.

On the financial side, revenue growth remains high (well above our high teens growth requirement), and in recent years, MELI has done an excellent job at growing profitably, reporting positive GAAP net profit in every consecutive quarter since the start of 2022.

We first covered the stock roughly one year ago, when we rated shares a Strong Buy, highlighting MELI's benefits of scale, strong consumer offerings, and robust business model. We also highlighted the company's shares, which we thought were trading at a relatively attractive valuation.

Fast-forward one year, and the stock has increased, if only modestly, underperforming the S&P 500:

It appears as though this underperformance is due to flat net profit growth over the last year, even though revenues have grown substantially.

To us, this lack of profit growth reflects understandable, strategic long-term investments made by the company

Today, in this article, we'll revisit MELI, highlight the valuation, and examine whether or not the company's stock still looks like a Strong Buy.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Financials

Over the last 12 months, MELI produced revenues of 26.2 billion and net income of 2.1 billion, making it one of the largest consumer marketplaces on earth:

It’s still substantially smaller than North American peer Amazon (AMZN), but the growth opportunity is also more robust.

Broadly, the Latin American consumer market remains largely under-penetrated in terms of online sales, and as more consumers and businesses shift from offline to online commerce, MercadoLibre stands poised to capture a significant role in this transaction volume.

On the consumer side, MercadoLibre operates an online marketplace, where the company itself—alongside third-party sellers and individuals—can list, sell, and fulfill products that are then shipped to end buyers.

On the finance side, MercadoLibre also operates a consumer finance division called 'Mercado Pago' that competes with other consumer finance companies in the region like Nubank (NU).

As Latin America transitions more towards online commerce and fulfillment, MELI stands to benefit—both with respect to transaction volume on its marketplace platform, as well as consumer payments and financing, which happen within its Mercado Pago app.

In recent quarters, the company has done an excellent job in continuing to monetize this opportunity, growing revenues at roughly 40% YoY:

Again, exactly one year ago, MELI was producing $17.4 billion in revenue. Now that number is almost $10 billion higher than 12 short months.

This is incredible growth, which has been driven by higher per-unit prices, along with a 26% YoY increase in unique active buyers to nearly 77 million:

Monthly active users of Mercado Pago also grew 29% YoY—particularly in Brazil and Mexico—as the value proposition for the app continues to improve:

At its core, this is a standard 'expanding market' growth story.

This total volume growth has multiple levers, and we're both encouraged by recent progress, and remain firm in our belief that the company will be able to drive continued top-line gains in the 20% plus region through 2029.

Analysts are currently projecting revenue growth of 36%, 28%, and 23% through the end of this year, next year, and 2027, and we think the company has a similar trajectory, but will actually see slightly less deterioration, maintaining at least 20% top-line sales growth through the end of the decade:

This all ladders up into earnings growth, which is what ultimately drives the stock.

In recent years, earnings were growing quite nicely after turning positive in 2021. Over the last four quarters, however, TTM net incomes have grown from $1.91 billion to $2.1 billion, reflecting falling gross margins, adverse currency losses, and higher tax expenses.

Over the last 12 months, it’s clear that the massive revenue growth we’ve seen has not translated to net income for shareholders:

The good news is the earnings appear set to expand rapidly in 2026 and 2027, by 54% and 37% YoY, respectively:

As investments made over the last 12 months begin to mature and earn ROI, we agree with analysts that MELI is on the verge of a massive multi-year run in EPS growth.

This is a serious, organic, growth opportunity.

Valuation

In return, the market is currently valuing MELI at roughly 52x blended earnings.

While this appears nominally expensive, we would argue that the EPS growth trajectory actually leads to a materially cheaper stock within a relatively short span of time.

With EPS set to grow from $41 to $86 over the next 24 months, the company's overall 'premium' multiple is actually about to get cut in half, dropping to only 24x, assuming 'average' estimates of company execution:

For a company this big and this dominant—growing top line at a 20% plus handle—26x earnings appears incredibly cheap, especially when earnings themselves are set to grow so rapidly through the end of the decade.

Analysts are currently expecting 24% YoY earnings per share growth or higher through the end of 2030, implying a forward price-to-earnings ratio of roughly 11X by the end of the decade.

While it’s possible that the market will value a strong, growing company like MercadoLibre at this multiple, it seems unlikely, and thus shareholders who invest now are aligning themselves with long-term organic growth in the stock that could produce high teens or even 20%+ annualized total returns for the foreseeable future, even when taking into account today’s valuation.

As a result, we think shares still look like a Strong Buy, despite the lack of recent progress.

Risks

There are considerable risks within investing in MercadoLibre, and it’s worth mentioning a few here so investors can get a complete picture.

First off, MercadoLibre faces competition on all fronts. Yes, the business is the most dominant consumer marketplace in Latin America, but there are other companies that will inevitably capture some market share in this growing opportunity.

Not only that, but NU, an incredibly popular consumer finance platform, will be aggressively competing for Latin American users in the years to come, directly impacting Mercado Pago.

These competition risks also highlight overall execution risks, as building physical delivery networks and coordinating the logistical nightmare remain no easy feat.

As a result, execution stumbles, competitive threats, and even unstable governments (at least in some MercadoLibre’s markets) represent risk factors to be aware of.

Summary

Overall, though, we think shares of MELI are attractive.

As the company grows EPS rapidly in the coming years, we expect that most of this growth will filter down to investors, even if the valuation contracts somewhat.

Thus our Strong Buy rating.

Cheers~

data.title: Nationwide Geneva Small Cap Growth Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Environment

2025 has marked one of the most speculative markets Geneva has experienced. The riskiest of equities related to Artificial Intelligence (AI), as well as a few tangential areas, are driving the market across the cap spectrum. We have only witnessed such market behavior three other times in the nearly 39 years Geneva has existed. The environment is also puzzling because on one hand, news has been dominated by concerns about tariffs, rising fiscal deficits, and political threats to Fed independence. On the other hand, every major asset class has delivered strong returns. Equity markets are at or near record highs, and credit spreads remain extremely tight. In Q3 specifically, US equities reached all-time highs and finished with an unusually strong September. The S&P 500® Index rose 3.5% in September (best September in 15 years) and there was some broadening of the market with the Russell 2000® Index up 12% in Q3 (best quarterly performance since Q4-23), although this was heavily biased towards lower-quality names.

In this juxtaposed market backdrop, investors may naturally ask what is driving these strong returns and how long can the rally continue? We believe the current market melt-up has been fueled by liquidity, easing financial conditions and investor enthusiasm for growth stories across new technology like AI. The composition of recent leading stocks, especially in the small-cap universe, points to speculation starting to rise as high-beta, unprofitable and low ROE companies outperformed heavily in Q3 after similar performance in Q2. More broadly, small- and mid-cap growth names did begin to catch up to the large caps after years of lagging as earnings slowly picked up within the Russell 3000® Index. The duration of this rally will depend on whether improving earnings and cash flow fundamentals can continue (likely will require AI to translate from capital outlays into return on investment) because historically, rallies led by lower quality factors tend to be fragile.

Performance Review

Investing in high-quality companies was a meaningful headwind to performance this quarter; within the broader US equity universe, low quality companies (those rated B or worse) returned 13.92% versus 3.50% for high quality (those rated B+ or better). In the Russell 2000 Growth Index, the trends were much the same – nonearners returned 22.3%, low ROE returned 25.3%, the highest beta names returned 24.4% and companies with a share price of less than $5 returned 32.0%. We’ve observed that the market is being driven by a handful of themes, these are AI & quantum computing, defense & space, metals & mining and crypto. In nearly all cases these companies don't meet our criteria for quality because they are not profitable (many are pre-revenue), have significant customer concentration risk, or the investor excitement doesn't match the fundamentals.

Outlook

Another key element of the market narrative in Q3 was continued AI optimism driving chipmakers, software names, power companies and infrastructure firms to outperform. Along with this, several other more “speculative” areas of the market outperformed including unprofitable, high short interest, metals/mining, bitcoin-sensitive, AI/quantum computing and defense/space. As we enter October, many of these themes remain robust, but some are starting to see increased scrutiny. One example is the recent announcement of a $100B investment by Nvidia into OpenAI to support datacenter buildouts with the notion that these centers would be populated with millions of NVDA's chips. This announcement accelerates concerns about "circularity" and “frothiness” in the AI ecosystem, which is reminiscent of the vendor financing issues which helped inflate the dot-com bubble of 1999. At the same time, there is starting to be more attention on AI monetization challenges, questionable productivity investments, and rising competitive pressures. While we don’t doubt this technology has the potential to change industries and increase productivity, the question is when and by how much? Can the unprecedented amount invested be ultimately justified by an appropriate return? In the latest Global Technology report from Bain & Co. they estimate there’s $2 trillion in new revenue needed to fund the AI infrastructure, which they believe it represents an $800B revenue shortfall even under optimistic assumptions. This is just one take, but few can argue the bar is very high. The other big question is who the ultimate beneficiaries will be. In the tech bubble, 90% of installed fiber went unused and the gains were ultimately accrued by the application layer within technology and by the well-run businesses across a multitude of sectors that became more productive by using the internet. This is not an uncommon pattern observed when new technologies evolve and it’s likely this time will be no different.

Overall, Q3’s story followed the path of least resistance to the upside, with still-solid economic data, improved earnings growth, and a resilient consumer combining with a Fed easing cycle and increasingly bullish technology sentiment to push major indices to several record highs. We acknowledge there is plenty of optimism in the short term from AI, consumer spending and upcoming fiscal and monetary stimulus, but eventually we believe the bill will come due. We have seen several of these more speculative markets before and while each situation is different, history generally rhymes. With valuations stretched and excess starting to enter the market, at some point—although it is very difficult to predict exactly when or how—we believe the market will correct seeing overbuilt capacity, unsustainable valuations and unprofitable companies. There have been five high-beta rallies with some similarities to the current period (1990-92, 1998-2000, 2002-04, 2009-10, 2020-21) and each ended with large high-beta underperformance of ~20% on average over the next 12 months once the markets turned. History has shown that over longer periods, company fundamentals do matter, and when we see an eventual market correction, quality-oriented managers should once again outperform.

data.title: Enel: Superb Returns, I Am Out (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
For about a month at this point, I have been focusing on increasing cash, divesting holdings, and selling off good-return companies. I was one of the very early investors in the European utility dip 2-3 years ago, back in 2022. You can find my early coverage here. And part of showing this to you is really my point in investing in a value-oriented manner. These are the rates of return I get in businesses that I invest in and then simply hold. Since 2022, my very early article that I almost "timed" perfectly, my rates of return were closing on 200%, and with dividends and FX, were actually there for the native ticker.

Sometimes you have to allow yourself to take a victory lap. That is what I am doing here - but also in combination with a warning that I am issuing, together with my downgrade.

The reason I am going to be using some strong wording here is that Enel (OTCPK:ENLAY) (OTCPK:ESOCF) was a very significant portion of my portfolio. At the investment, over 2%. At the sale, almost 5.5% in total. For my portfolio size, that is huge, as I usually do not have positions larger than 3%. It also means that a massive amount of capital was sitting in this position.

Unfortunately, as of November, I could no longer defend the valuation or the upside of the company.

Selling a position like this is not easy, one that I have covered here most recently.

These are the returns that I am going for, in an ideal situation. Fundamentally safe companies that go "nowhere" in terms of risk, but that can go everywhere in terms of upside. Enel was one such company. Enel is not that company any longer at this valuation.

It is difficult to sell because there are a lot of prospective future dividends at incredibly attractive YoCs tied to the position. My YoC was close to double digits for this investment, thanks to recent slow increases. But at heart, valuation matters more than dividends because the prospect of losing out on that 200%+ RoR is not something that I am willing to do.

So, I am rotating, and unless you have some very positive expectations about the company, I believe it is a good time to do the same.

Let me show you why.

Enel - Why Upside From Here Is Unlikely And Would Require A Significant Premium

The multinational Enel is attractive based on its distribution of electricity and gas, with a leg up on renewables, and a strong focus. To argue that this company will not grow further requires me to show you why growth and/or further premiumization is unlikely. So I will do that.

Since I bought the company in 2022, the company has focused on a plan of strategic growth, combined with asset sales worth €15B. This included accelerating decarbonization, which it has done. It included storage and renewables, which it is doing and has also done. It expects a 98% renewable energy portion by 2025E, and works towards net-zero by 2040. Much of the strategic progress expected has been delivered, and much earlier than expected.

By divesting now, I am saying that the company's future plans for growth will not result in as big a bump in earnings growth or valuation growth as the historical ones have. By divesting now, I am also saying that I believe it is unlikely for the market to further premiumize the company's valuation here.

Enel has significantly improved its leverage to where it is now one of the lowest-leveraged utilities in Europe, at less than 2.6x. This lowers financing costs while increasing stability.

It makes you wonder why the company was "low" in the first place. The reason for that is simple - it acquired Endesa at the top of the valuation and credit cycle in 2008, pre-GFC. This really shows you what can happen if you are not cognizant of external factors, and your focus is very narrow. The sovereign debt crises were, of course, connected in both Spain and Italy, but it would take over 8 years for recovery to even become a "thing".

What I like about the new management, under Flavio Cattaneo, is that he takes a far more conservative view of the renewable investment plan. The company's investment targets here have been slashed. This means two things.

First, CapEx and investments will be lower than expected. The disposals have shored up the balance sheet, and given its experience in GFC, I doubt very much that Enel will be making the same mistake twice. Secondly, however, this means that the operational upside and efficiencies are more limited.

In layman's terms, the company will have a harder time growing earnings. And this is already visible in forecasts. Current EPS growth forecasts are at, or below, inflation levels, between lows of 2.24% and highs of 3.5%. This is, in my view, insufficient for significant premiumization and only adds to the reason why I am selling.

I want to make it crystal clear that the sole reason I am rotating Enel is the company's valuation. If the company traded below where it does now, I would not be selling; I might buy more as long as it was "kind of" attractive to own it here. I do not mind holding companies for yield or stability. But when it is stability, yield, and now growth at a high valuation, it becomes a much harder argument.

The latest results, 9M, were good, but they do not change the thesis here either. There is, in my view, nothing in the company that does.

The combination of sitting on a significant return, and "knowing", as I will show you in valuation that I believe I do, that the company is unlikely to outperform here, forces my hand based on my tenets of investment.

Valuation For Enel - The Only Problem Is Here

Let me start by saying that for Enel to be a "BUY", you need to believe that the company deserves a 14-15x P/E premium. This is not a problem in itself, necessarily - the problem with that is that Enel has not consistently traded at 15x P/E or close to it for any long period of time.

The 20-year average, which I tend to use for Utilities because utilities are the "best part of boring", shows us a 12.4x P/E average. The current average at €8.96 is 13.2x, which is meaningfully above that average. The yield is now 5.25%, which is good, and a BBB credit, which is also good.

Enel is not an unsafe company - it is just an unattractively valued company. If we assume the 12.41x P/E, which implies a fair value of €9/share, we get a 5.67% return per year, and less than 0.55% without the yield.

Let me argue a few points against why the company is unlikely to see EPS growth beyond this, as well as why premiumization is unwarranted. The company's income is largely from LATAM, Italy, and Spain, which are volatile, and as we have seen above and will see in risks, geographies that may not offer the returns and rates that even I expected. Based on this, I forecast no higher than an EBITDA growth of 1.4% per year until 2029E due to significant grid investments and more renewables. Because over 30% of the company's income is subject to volatility (LATAM, Italy, Spain), and also includes significant hydro assets, the risk/reward ratio here is looking increasingly unfavorable. That does not even include volatility from FX, which, given the company's LATAM exposure, is also not trivial.

At this valuation, the risks that I had previously considered acceptable relative to the price I paid at 4/share and below (remember, I bought my first stake below €4) are no longer appealing.

I give the company a PT of €7/share, with a fair value of €9/share, citing a 12.4x P/E average for the fair value (20-year average), and citing lack of growth as a reason for not moving from my PT. That means the company is now above my fair value target.

Alternatives for Enel exist, but the unfortunate news for cash-heavy investors is that I do not consider any of those attractive either. We are talking about E.ON (OTCPK:EONGY), RWE (OTCPK:RWEOY), maybe Iberdrola (OTCPK:IBDRY) - all of them are overvalued. I view the utility space as too crowded right now, and not something I would look at investing in.

Watching the market peak has been a very sobering experience. Unlike most investors, who seem to be "chomping at the bit" to get more of some companies, I generally tend to go against the grain in all things. When most people are calling not to buy something, you can usually find me doing research on exactly that.

When people, on the other hand, are shouting at the heavens to buy X and not look back, you can usually find me looking for the emergency exit.

That latter scenario is more or less where I see myself right now. I have not had this much cash for over a decade, and I have no big investment plans in the near term. I view this market as very inflated and risky, even if not all sectors are necessarily at that peak. But when the market turns, I believe it will take most things down alongside it.

So, for the time being, I am exiting investments that have reached their potential. This, dear readers, is one.

I am now out of it. Risks are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

Risks for the company are certainly clearer now - and I am talking about more than simply valuation levels. With allowed network returns under the eye of regulators, I am specifically worried about the 2026-2031 period in Spain. Why? Because the Spanish electricity and gas return rates are at less than 7%, which was the investor's expectation. It is even below my estimate of 6.8%, which I thought was conservative. But instead, Spain ended at 6.58%, which is well below even bearish expectations.

The company, through its lobbyist Aelec, even wanted 7.5%, which means a close to 100 bps difference at that low a base rate. I believe the risk here is actually being underreported in what it could do to Enel's top-line and EPS. Anyone providing an article without taking into consideration what this could mean long-term for premiumization is in danger of being too positive, in my book.

Apart from that, EPS growth estimates of 2-3% might even be too positive, and this brings into question whether the company could really grow its dividend as expected.

On the basis of this, I give the following thesis.

Thesis

My plans for holding Enel are simple. Buy it cheap - and hold it until/if it gets overvalued - which it can, and now is. Cash in on an inflation-beating dividend until then. This was my plan in 2022, and it has materialized and come full circle. I am rotating it.

There are now risks for Enel, and I consider these to be more relevant than whatever upside remains. If you are still interested in investing in the company here, remember the relevant ADR here - ENLAY. You can also choose to invest in specific Enel business activities - this is not what that article is about, and I have not gone deep enough to consider individual segments like the Americas, Chile, or Russia as their own investments. This thesis is for Enel as a whole, not any of its constituent parts.

Updated for 2026-2027E, the company is expected to generate stable returns and EPS growth, but close to flat development as the CapEx peters out a bit, and with the changed strategy, I believe flat and anemic development to be far more likely than before. This thesis has, because of this, reached its conclusion, and the "dot" in this case is the valuation.

I see no reason to give the company a higher P/E, and I downgrade my rating to "HOLD". I have sold over 5% of my portfolio in this company during November, and am completely out for the time being. The company is above my stated PT of €7/share, and I do not see a reason to raise it here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically is not enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of €7/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that is attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of November 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Truist Bank Preferreds Reviewed: All Rated Hold
data.description:
Introduction

Truist Financial Corporation (TFC) was the result of SunTrust and BB&T banks merging in late 2019. Like many, I wondered how the combined bank chose that name. It combines the concepts of "true" and "trust" to reflect a shared belief that the merged banks are building a better future for clients and the communities they serve. While regional, Truist ranks in the Top 10 U.S. banks based on Total Assets.

The bank has the following $25 Par preferred stocks available, all of which are compared here.

  • Truist Financial Corp Series I Non-Cumulative Perpetual Preferred Stock (TFC.PR.I).
  • Truist Financial Corp. 5.25% Dep Shares Series O Non-Cumul Perpetual Prfd Stock (TFC.PR.O).
  • Truist Financial Corp 4.75% Dep Shares Series R Non-Cumulative Preferred Stock (TFC.PR.R).

Based on the yields and what is available from other regional banks with slightly higher risks, I am giving all three issues a Hold rating.

Truist Financial Corporation review

Seeking Alpha describes this bank as (edited):

Truist Financial Corporation, a financial services company, provides banking and trust services in the Southeastern and Mid-Atlantic United States. The company operates through two segments, Consumer and Small Business Banking; and Wholesale Banking. Its deposit products include noninterest-bearing checking, interest-bearing checking, savings, and money market deposit accounts. The company also provides funding; asset management; automobile lending; credit card lending; consumer finance; home equity and mortgage lending; other direct retail lending; home mortgage lending. The company was formerly known as BB&T Corporation and changed its name to Truist Financial Corporation in December 2019. Truist Financial Corporation was founded in 1872 and is headquartered in Charlotte, North Carolina.

The Investor Relations website describes the bank as such:

Truist Financial Corporation is a purpose-driven company dedicated to building a better future for its clients, teammates and communities. With 275 years of combined BB&T and SunTrust history, Truist is one of the nation's largest financial services holding companies offering a wide range of services including retail, small business and commercial banking; asset management; capital markets; commercial real estate; corporate and institutional banking; insurance; mortgage; payments; specialized lending and wealth management. Headquartered in Charlotte, North Carolina, Truist serves approximately 10 million households with leading market share in many high-growth markets in the country.

Source: ir.truist.

In the most recent earnings presentation, the PDF opens with this, which defines the purpose, mission, and values of the bank.

The loan portfolio is diversified, though 50% are in commercial and industrial loans.

Truist has just under $400B in deposits, all except the Time Deposits can be requested by customers without notice.

Truist has $544B in Total Assets, making it the 8th largest bank by that measure in the U.S.

I look to the Income Statement, to find how well Net Income is covering the money needed to pay all the preferred dividends. Here that ratio is 15X, well above what I have seen in other regional banks.

For its ability to redeem all the preferreds, I use the Total Common Equity, or TCE, over PFD Par. That ratio is 10X, also very strong.

Preferred stocks compared

I compiled relevant data points in the table below.

Factor TFC I TFC O TFC R
Issued BBT issue 5/19/20 7/30/20
Size (MM shares) 20 20 37
Coupon Variable* 5.25% 4.75%
Price $21.70 $22.63 $19.83
Yield 5.6% 5.8% 6.0%
Call date 12/15/24 6/1/25 9/1/25

* The series I uses the 3-mo SOFR + 26.151 bps risk adjustment + 53bps fixed, subject to a 400 bps floor: 3-mo SOFR is 3.9% today.

  • All three issues carry a BBB- rating, are non-cumulative, and qualify for the preferential tax rates.
  • All three are past their Call dates.
  • The O series has the best odds of being Called based on the coupons unless SOFR climbs above 4.5%.
  • The I series formula would need a 3-mo SOFR above 4% to logically be Called before the R series.
  • While the 4% floor is good, the add-on is very poor compared to other floating-rate bank issues.
  • How safe these issues are is reflected in the low yields they currently offer.

Based on the yields and what is available from other regional banks with slightly higher risks, I am giving all three issues a Hold rating.

Portfolio strategy

When interest rates are declining, I favor fixed over floating coupons, maybe except for floaters with a high floor like the TFC I series. Then I look for one with either long Call protection or Call protection provided by higher coupon issues from the same issuer, which logic says should get Called first, assuming eligible. Of course, yield and default/non-payment risk must still be considered during the investor’s due diligence process. My desired yield, or YTC, is based on how risky I view the issuer, which I start that evaluation using the TCE/PFD value ratio.

Investors need to look at how preferred stocks come with different features, such as these:

  • Fixed or floating coupon: While fixed provides greater income certainty, floating provides better price protection when rates are climbing.
  • Perpetual or Term: There are preferred stocks that mature.
  • Call protection/risk: The decision here is how long you want to be protected from the possibility of having your issue taken away.
  • Default/bankruptcy protection: The capital ranking (bonds, preferreds, common) comes into play based on one's risk tolerance.
  • Cumulative or not: Be cumulative gives investors protection in case a dividend payment is skipped.

Final note

Truist has several $1000 Par preferred stocks, one of which was just Called.

Truist Financial Corporation (the "Company") announced today that it has decided to call all of the outstanding Series P Depositary Shares related to its outstanding 4.950% Series P Fixed Rate Reset Non-Cumulative Perpetual Preferred Stock (CUSIP #89832QAE9) (the "Series P Depositary Shares") for redemption on November 13, 2025 (the "Redemption Date"). The redemption price will be US$1,000.00 per Series P Depositary Share, plus declared and unpaid dividends up to but excluding the Redemption Date (the "Redemption Price"). On and after the Redemption Date, the Series P Depositary Shares so redeemed will no longer be deemed to be outstanding, dividends on such Series P Depositary Shares will cease to accumulate and all rights of the holders of such Series P Depositary Shares will cease, except for the right to receive the Redemption Price.

The Series P Depositary Shares will be redeemed from the Company's retained earnings.

Source: Call notice.

Best I could determine, there are two other $1000 Par issues outstanding with coupons of 4.8% (N series) and 5.1% (Q series, not Call eligible).

data.title: Virtus Large Cap Growth SMA Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Portfolio Review

The Silvant Large Cap Growth SMA gained +8.90% (gross)/+8.09% (net) for the quarter compared to the Russell 1000 Growth Index’s return of +10.51%. Consumer discretionary and healthcare stock selection detracted from performance. Communication services stock selection benefited returns.

AppLovin and Alphabet were among the top stock contributors to relative performance.

  • Digital advertising and app technology firm AppLovin (APP) rose on rapidly expanding revenue and profit gains that exceeded market expectations, after adjusting for a recent divestment of its mobile gaming business. The company also raised its forward guidance. Initially purchased in the first quarter and increased in the second, the position has been a strong performer for the Strategy. The company remains in the investment stages of growing its business but is on track to deliver positive operating income and earnings per share this year, supported by continued business acceleration and expanding market share.
  • Leading technology conglomerate Alphabet (GOOGL) posted another strong quarter, beating both revenue and earnings expectations. The company continued to drive AI integration and monetization through its Gemini family of multimodal large language models across multiple segments of its business, enhancing results and user experience. Cloud demand, another key metric, also saw strong gains. We maintained the position given these positive tailwinds, while monitoring the company’s substantial capital expenditures, projected at $75-$85 billion in 2025 and expected to rise further in 2026.

Fair Isaac Corp and Intuitive Surgical were among the largest stock detractors from relative performance.

  • Fair Isaac Corp. (FICO), best known or its FICO consumer credit score, delivered solid financial results but lagged due to regulatory pressures on its longstanding near-monopoly, particularly in the mortgage scoring market. Historically, FICO has been able to raise prices without attracting much scrutiny, but recent government efforts to increase competition have cast uncertainty over its pricing power. While the company’s underlying business remains strong, investor concern over potential erosion of its dominant position weighed on the stock. We continued to hold the position but are closely monitoring it.
  • Surgical robotics firm Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) also underperformed despite reporting a solid quarter. Procedure growth, a key metric, remained strong, and the launch of da Vinci 5, the company’s flagship system, outpaced expectations, though the key metric of overall system placements was slightly soft due to its international rollout delay. The stock was also pressured by concerns over third-party refurbished instruments, system trade and impact on gross margins with a shift to the full da Vinci 5 launch, and a broader sector rotation out of medical technology. We continued to hold the stock, as we expect these issues will be transitory.

Portfolio Changes

There were no new positions. The Trade Desk (TTD), a programmatic advertising technology company that helps users buy digital ads efficiently, was the only exit due to decelerating growth and market share losses, both key metrics for the Strategy.

Outlook

Looking ahead, growth markets largely remain supported by solid fundamentals, although periods of digestion can be common after repeatedly reaching new highs. Strong capital expenditures and consumer spending, particularly among high-end buyers, continue to drive revenue, earnings, and free cash flow growth across many companies. Market broadening in the third quarter, including gains beyond tech and AI, also provides a healthier and more diversified backdrop. On the cautionary side, tighter immigration policy may weigh on the labor market, while sticky inflation and tariffs could create future pricing and economic pressures once fully reflected in the data. Unfortunately, the U.S. government shutdown adds a layer of opacity, making it harder to gauge these risks. While the upcoming earnings season will be key to confirming current momentum, the overall positive economic environment supports cautious optimism that markets could continue to extend gains, even as investors navigate these potential headwinds.

Growth Is A Condition

Silvant deploys a fundamental, bottom-up, team-based approach to investing in growth stocks that we believe can exceed investors expectations and outperform the Russell 1000 Growth Index over rolling three- to five-year cycles.

Our approach is three-fold:

Growth is a Condition

  • Any company can grow
  • Cast a wider net
  • Secular, cyclical, non-traditional
  • Own all major index sectors

Stock-Specific Alpha

  • Identify primary business drivers
  • 3-6 company fundamentals
  • Track and move with the data
  • Buy and sell when key metrics change

Style Agnostic

  • Avoid typical style bias
  • Diversify thematic risks
  • Minimize behavioral bias
  • Avoid macro timing calls

We invest in companies that offer specific conditions for growth and we believe can exceed investor expectations driven by the fundamental inflections of their most important metrics. Applying this approach across companies in all the major index sectors helps keep the portfolio’s risk at the stock-specific level, making our process more consistent, repeatable, and transparent for our clients.

Effective January 1, 2025, the minimum portfolio size for the Large Cap Growth Wrap Strategy Composite is $100,000. Prior to 1/1/2025, the minimum portfolio size for the Composite was $1,000,000.
  4. Benchmark. The benchmark for the Large Cap Growth Wrap Strategy Composite is the Russell 1000 Growth Index, which measures the performance of those Russell 1000 companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher forecasted growth values. Index returns do not reflect the deduction of any fees or expenses.
  5. Returns and Fees. Returns are calculated based on total return which (a) includes cash returns, realized and unrealized gains plus income, and reinvestment of dividends and other earnings; (b) utilizes trade date and accrual accounting; and (c) is after the deduction of actual trading fees and expenses. Portfolio returns are calculated utilizing daily valuation. Composite returns, calculated monthly, are the weighted average return of the underlying portfolios using beginning-of-period market values. Composite gross of fee returns do not reflect any deduction for investment advisory fees, custodial charges, or other costs that a client might incur in connection with the management of an account. Investment advisory fees are described in Part 2 of the firm’s Form ADV. Prior to September 1, 2015, the gross of fee return for the mutual fund was calculated by adding back the fund’s published total operating expense ratio to the net of fee mutual fund performance; we now use the gross of fee return of the underlying portfolio. Net returns are calculated using the wrap fee, which includes all charges for portfolio management, custody and other administrative fees and are calculated by subtracting the highest applicable wrap fee (3.0% on an annual basis, or .25% monthly), on a monthly basis from the gross composite monthly return. Individual results may vary due to market conditions, trading costs, account-specific factors such as varying account minimums, and other unique circumstances.
  6. Fee Schedule. The investment management fee schedule for the wrap programs vary between 1.25% and 3.00%. Bundled fees may vary by Sponsor. The client is referred to the Program Sponsor’s ADV 2A for a full disclosure of the fee schedule.
  7. Ex Post Standard Deviation. The three-year annualized standard deviation measures the variability of the composite gross returns, and the benchmark returns over the preceding 36-month period. No three-year annualized standard deviations are reported for periods less than 3 years (shown as N/A).
  8. Composite Dispersion. The dispersion of annual or YTD returns is measured by the equal-weighted standard deviation of portfolio gross returns represented within the composite for the full year or period. No dispersion is reported for periods with five or fewer portfolios (shown as N/A).
  9. Currency. Valuations and returns are computed and stated in U.S. Dollars.
  10. Composite Creation and Inception Date. This composite was created on January 1, 2025 and has performance history with an inception date of April 1, 2007.
  11. Significant Cash Flow Policy. Beginning March 31, 2008, composite policy required the temporary removal of any portfolio incurring a client initiated significant cash inflow or outflow of 10% or greater of portfolio assets. This policy was discontinued effective January 1, 2025.
  12. Availability of Information. Policies for valuing investments, calculating performance, and preparing GIPS Reports are available upon request. A list of all composite investment strategies offered by the firm, with a description of each strategy, is available upon request. The type of portfolios in which each strategy is available (segregated account, limited distribution pooled fund, or broad distribution pooled fund) is indicated in the description of each strategy.

data.title: My 3 Essential Market Truths Every Investor Needs To Know Right Now
data.description:
Introduction

I have to admit that it seems like market tensions are high. That’s despite sky-high stock prices. The other day, I was listening to a podcast from CNBC, where someone (I forgot who it was) said something along the lines that "investors are afraid of the dark," meaning that regardless of how much capital gains we may have enjoyed, even the whiff of bad times is enough to get people scared.

Based on that context, I just read something on Yahoo Finance, which included a quote from Seneca, who was a philosopher in Ancient Rome. His most famous quote is the one below:

"We suffer more often in imagination than reality." - Seneca

The article noted that despite rising layoffs, horrible cyclical growth, AI-related disruption fears, and the government shutdown, the market is doing just fine.

If anything, I think it’s fair to say that most sell-offs hit us when we least expect them. Just think of the pandemic. I’m sure that many people were nervous in 2019, yet none of them could have known that the one thing sinking the market would be a virus in 2020.

And even if people predicted it, how many do you think would have predicted that the aftermath of that virus would be extremely bullish for the market?

That said, a bit of anxiety isn’t just human, but it’s also a benefit, according to a 2021 article from two clinical psychologists that I just found. If we’re too relaxed, we’re not sufficiently motivated. If we’re too anxious, we cannot focus. The sweet spot in between is just enough worry to keep us on our toes for "peak arousal and performance."

But that’s not why I am writing this.

My point is that general anxiety seems to be high because we are dealing with so many factors that all could have a major market impact, both positive and negative.

That’s what makes people nervous, and I see it in the comments every day.

I always have the silly comparison of staying in a great hotel on vacation. We should be feeling great, but not knowing if the weather over the next few days will be good or bad could cause some anxiety. Again, it’s silly, but you know what I mean.

Hence, I decided to work my way through what I consider the three most important market factors right now, including the market's structure, critical liquidity, and what could be a substantial cyclical recovery.

To me, this is a very important basis for future investments.

So, let's dive in!

It’s An AI-Driven Bull Market With Collapsing Breadth

The stock market has a high valuation. By now, that may not come as a surprise to anyone.

A >23.0x forward P/E ratio leaves little room for error, as it’s not only one of the highest numbers in decades (especially if we ignore the pandemic), but also based on solid growth expectations, as the LTM P/E ratio of the market is roughly 30.0x. The difference between forward and LTM is forward earnings growth expectations.

The good news is that earnings growth is solid. As reported by The Wall Street Journal, more than 80% of the 446 S&P 500 companies that have reported earnings so far have beaten expectations. That’s the highest number since early 2021.

Interestingly enough, it hasn’t benefited them much. According to the same source I just used, the S&P 500 is up roughly 1.3% since October 14, when earnings season was kicked off by the big banks. Companies that beat expectations beat this return by just 0.3%.

This may seem like a meaningless statistic. However, I believe it’s a crucial part of my thesis, as the market seems to be too top-heavy. See, as I have often discussed, the market is super top-heavy, as the biggest ten stocks of the index account for more than 40% of its weighting, one of the highest numbers ever.

The gap has rapidly widened since AI became mainstream more than two years ago, as the charts of the market-cap weighted S&P 500 and the equal-weight S&P 500 show below.

According to Société Générale calculations, reported by Bloomberg, the index would be close to 8,000 points if non-AI stocks had kept up with the market.

Using the same source, we find that things have gone so far that the Nasdaq 100 accounts for 82% of the S&P 500’s 12% EPS growth, which shows how concentrated this market has become.

So, to put everything we discussed so far in context, it seems the market has rallied too far too quickly, giving it signs of exhaustion. That’s also why it seems the market has been much harder on some of the Big Tech earnings, as the WSJ reported:

When Facebook parent Meta Platforms announced record quarterly sales on Oct. 29, the stock tumbled 11% the following day. Why? Because the company revealed that its AI spending plans, already eye-watering, would only increase in 2026.

Then, on Nov. 3, data analytics company Palantir reported record revenue and lifted its revenue guidance for the full year, adding that it expected its U.S. commercial business to more than double. The stock promptly slid 8%. - WSJ

Now, it seems like we have entered an environment where investors require proof of AI success. I’m saying that because Amazon (AMZN), which reported accelerating cloud computing growth and AI adoption, rallied hard after earnings, while Meta Platforms (META), which has very limited mass-adoption AI products, got sold off rather severely.

To put that into perspective, we're now double the power capacity that AWS was in 2022, and we're on track to double again by 2027. [...] We've recently brought Project Rainier online, our massive AI compute cluster spanning multiple U.S. data centers and containing nearly 500,000 of our Trainium2 chips. Anthropic is using it now to build and deploy its industry-leading AI model, Claude, which we expect to be on more than 1 million Trainium2 chips by year-end. Trainium2 continues to see strong adoption, is fully subscribed, and is now a multibillion-dollar business that grew 150% quarter-over-quarter. - AMZN 3Q25

To show you how quickly things can go, the chart below shows the ratio between META stock and the S&P 500. After reaching an all-time high, the recent decline erased nearly two years of outperformance, as the ratio fell to early 2024 levels.

I am not saying that META is dead money. My point is that the Mag-7 are under increasing scrutiny, as investors know these giants are increasingly going after each other, as spending trillions in long-term CapEx is all about getting AI leadership.

That’s the tricky part and something I truly cannot mention enough.

Liquidity Will Become A Much Bigger Story

John Authers wrote in a recent Bloomberg article (the one I just mentioned) that money supply is becoming a huge factor. He used a quote from Warren Buffett, who said that once the tide goes out, we find out who has been swimming naked.

This may apply to the massive amount of M2 money supply in the market, which has risen by roughly $4 trillion since the start of the 2020 pandemic, as we can see below.

Higher liquidity means that more capital has the potential to chase stocks, bonds, real estate, and other assets. That’s why there are usually three things to keep in mind when dealing with liquidity, according to SJB Global:

  • Spotting Market Trends: If M2 is climbing fast, there’s usually more fuel for a stock rally. If it’s shrinking, it might be time to get more defensive.
  • Understanding Cycles: M2 can sometimes be a better indicator of market direction than traditional metrics like earnings or GDP. Watching liquidity trends helps financial planners stay ahead of the curve.
  • Adjusting Portfolios: When M2 is rising, growth-oriented assets like stocks and real estate might make sense. When it’s falling, shifting toward bonds, cash, or commodities could be a safer play.

As part of my "running it hot" thesis, which foresees a scenario where both the Fed and the government accept higher inflation if it allows them to cause broadening economic growth and strengthen cyclical sectors like housing and manufacturing, I believe liquidity will remain very bullish.

That’s where the Financial Times comes in, which wrote "Why the Fed’s balance sheet may grow again" a few days ago. It was about Joseph Wang, who is one of the smartest people when it comes to the Fed, due to his career as an open market trader with years of expertise in this field. I think some of you may have heard his name before.

Anyway, he makes the case that the Fed will have to expand its balance sheet again to either support risk assets from collapsing, keep yields low, or even avoid a liquidity crisis.

His main point has to do with the high deficit, which is being funded through a lot of short-term debt. Initially, this was the Yellen playbook under Biden. Now, Treasury Secretary Bessent is doing the same. This means the TGA (that’s the Treasury General Account, or the government’s checking account) needs to hold a lot more liquidity due to frequent debt maturities.

Interestingly enough, a high cash balance sucks capital out of the system. After all, capital on the TGA isn’t in other places of the economy, to put it in simple terms. For example, the collapse in the TGA’s balance in 2021 was extremely bullish. That was also the case in 2023.

That’s also why the repo market is now running at $3 trillion a day, which means investors are borrowing capital using treasuries they already own as collateral to buy more short-term debt. That’s one way to finance short-term debt, as weird as it may sound.

Hence, more liquidity is needed for stability reasons. Wang estimates the Fed may have to expand its balance sheet by up to $500 billion per year. QE in 2021 was $1.4 trillion. Now, it’s much lower, yet I think it perfectly confirms we are about to see way more liquidity.

But Wang’s mention of perception is important here. If the Fed does crank up the printing press again, it will surely be seen as risk positive, a "Fed put", whether or not the money added to the financial system actually pushes up asset prices mechanically. “Don’t fight the Fed” is a self-fulfilling prophecy a lot of the time. - Financial Times

While this thesis isn’t based on "running it hot," it perfectly fits my own view, as I think the Fed will be a liquidity tailwind again, which, historically speaking, is bullish for risk assets, cyclical economic growth, and inflation.

Cyclical Growth Looks To Be Bottoming

This is, by far, my riskiest thesis, as I have explained in a number of articles this year.

It’s risky because this thesis is based on investment ideas at the long end of the Equity Yield Curve, as I discussed on Sunday. Essentially, I am investing in high-quality companies and a thesis that is extremely unloved right now. While that makes for a great risk/reward (I think), it also means I have to deal with elevated volatility and downside risk until I’m right (assuming I will be right).

In recent months, I believe we have started to witness green shoots.

One big part of this thesis is the lagging effect of data center spending. So far, this buildout story has mainly benefited infrastructure, cooling, chips, and other related players. And that’s obviously entirely justified. Jordi Visser, who has been on top of this for a while, said in a recent video that this is not the time to be bearish, as accelerating data center spending will now broaden economic growth, as QE-like spending works its way through the economy.

I think the chart below captures this beautifully.

Visser also makes the case that this wasn’t even a big bullish factor since this year’s deregulation through the One Big Beautiful Bill. I agree with that, as it kick-started a whole range of new projects.

On top of that, I believe the aforementioned liquidity tailwinds from the Fed, easing global trade war risks, and the general focus on housing from the government (think of the 50-year mortgage initiative) are all bullish factors.

While the ISM Manufacturing Index is still below 50, we can assume that this is mainly due to domestic issues like tariffs and government shutdown uncertainties. As these are fading, I think we will see a similar development to what we saw in the S&P Global U.S. Manufacturing PMI, which saw the highest demand growth in 20 months.

Again, it’s a green shoot, but if this is the start of a trend, I believe the risk/reward of cyclical stocks is terrific. It could even be the start of a much stronger rotation.

This is also why I expect to spend a lot more time on this subject, as it has a wide range of implications:

  • It could be very bullish for cyclical stocks.
  • This includes growth- and income-focused investments.
  • Most of these investments have gone nowhere since 2022 (think of Union Pacific (UNP), which I always use as an example due to its high correlation with the ISM Manufacturing Index).

  • If the top-heavy market feels the headwind from hyperscaler weakness, it will be easier for capital to rotate if growth in other S&P 500 areas is rebounding.

In other words, being right about this wouldn’t just be important for my portfolio but would be a crucial relief for the top-heavy market.

So far, it has been a bumpy ride, but I’m very happy with these developments.

Takeaway

If you’re one of the people who get anxious in this market environment, I fully understand that. We are truly dealing with a wide range of major developments, all of which could be either very bullish or very bearish, depending on your view of things.

The good news is that very far under the surface, I think there’s plenty of room for optimism.

That’s why I wrote this article, as I see three major developments.

First, we’re dealing with a top-heavy market that is on increasingly shaky ground, as investors doubt the payoff that some companies may receive from aggressive AI spending. When adding their lofty valuations, the ice is getting thinner.

However, point two may come to the rescue, as we could see liquidity benefits from the Fed for the first time since it started tightening. This could be a tailwind for risk assets, inflationary, and good news for point three.

Point three is rebounding cyclical growth. While it’s still a fragile thesis, new developments continue to support a bottom, fueled by less uncertainty, less regulation, and QE-like data center spending, among other factors.

In other words, despite all uncertainty, I think there’s plenty of opportunity in this market. We just need to carefully assess the biggest drivers and connect the dots.

That’s what I will continue to do.

Risks To My Thesis

The biggest risk is that I’m flat-out wrong. In this specific case, the biggest risk would be a recession. It would end the cyclical recovery thesis, force the Fed to ease even more, and potentially cause the Mag-7 to dominate the market even more, as it could mean AI remains the only thesis that really works.

Easing prices

The Trump administration plans to remove tariffs on certain goods from four Latin American countries that are not produced in the U.S. The planned tariff relief aims to address high food prices and reduce grocery bills for consumers at home.

The details: The White House announced agreements with Ecuador, Argentina, Guatemala and El Salvador to deepen bilateral trade and investment. The four countries agreed to address a range of non-tariff barriers for U.S. goods and businesses. Notably, Argentina opened its market to live cattle from the U.S. and committed to allow market access for U.S. poultry within a year. "The deals will help U.S. farmers, ranchers, fishermen, small businesses, and manufacturers to increase U.S. exports to and expand business opportunities with these trading partners," the White House declared.

In return, bananas, coffee, beef, cocoa, and certain textile products imported from these countries will be exempt from tariffs, a senior U.S. official told reporters. U.S. tariff rates on other imports from these nations remain the same - 15% for Ecuador; and 10% for Argentina, Guatemala and El Salvador. The deals are expected to be signed within roughly two weeks. The White House expects U.S. retailers and wholesalers to pass on any benefits to American consumers.

Bigger picture: "Heightened political concerns about U.S. consumers' inflation perceptions seem to be leading a drive to reduce the tariffs U.S. importers pay on food products," said Paul Donovan, UBS' chief economist. "U.S. consumers' belief that they pay for tariffs is likely to be reinforced by this stance." According to CPI data, banana prices rose about 8% on average from January to September, while coffee got 15% more expensive.
