data.title: PayPal: A Solid GARP Stock Trapped In Consolidation (Technical Analysis And Downgrade)
data.description:
PYPL Stock: Strong Q3 Results Met With Lukewarm Market Response

I last covered PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL) on 9/5. That analysis was triggered by the new treasury stock holdings the company reported as part of its FQ2 earnings report, and I concluded with a Strong Buy rating. Since then, there have been a few new catalysts evolving around the firm. In the remainder of this article, I will detail my thoughts on the top 2 as I see them: the company's FQ3 earnings report (ER) and the technical trading patterns following the ER.

As a brief recap, PayPal delivered a robust ER (released on Oct 28) for the third fiscal quarter for 2025. As seen from the following snapshot, the earnings beat analyst expectations on all metrics. Non-GAAP EPS dialed in at $1.34, and total revenues grew to $8.4 billion. Total Payment Volume (TPV) also enjoyed a robust growth of around 8% YOY. Thanks to the strong operational performance, the ER also raised the full-year EPS guidance. Despite all the positives, the market's responses were lukewarm at best, as the stock prices moved sideways in reaction, as detailed in the next section.

PYPL's Consolidation Is Likely to Continue

After analyzing its latest trading indicators (as shown in the next chart), I believe PYPL is in and will continue to be in a technical consolidation for the near term. To start, the current share price of $65.57 is now hovering slightly beneath the key 20-day Moving Average (MA20) at $68.28, whose trend has become quite flat since my last writing. Secondly, the trading volume has also been decreasing since the announcement of the ER. As seen, the stock witnessed significant trading shortly after the ER - as usually is the case for most stocks. Trading volume peaked at nearly 90 million shares following the ER, as highlighted in the blue box. However, trading activities quickly dissipated over the next few days. As of this writing, the daily trading volume shrank to about 6.4 million shares only, the lowest levels since the release of the ER.

As a third classical consolidation signal, the Relative Strength Index (RSI) has also flattened out lately and has been hovering around the neutral zone of 50. Currently, the reading is 42.40, signaling a lack of strong conviction from either buyers or sellers needed to initiate a definite trend.

Finally, before leaving this chart, I want to draw your attention to the price-volume bar highlighted by the red rectangle. This rectangle highlights the price range that has attracted the heaviest trading volumes in recent months. As such, I consider this price range of around $68 to $69 a key resistance level for the stock. PYPL's current stock price hovers slightly below this resistance level. With the muted trading volume and the business uncertainties ahead (more on this in the risk section), I anticipate it will take some time for the large number of investors who participated in this trading window to be replaced by more bullish ones (or become more bullish themselves).

PYPL Stock: A Solid GARP Candidate

Although for investors with a longer timeframe, the stock is appealing. I see attractive capital gains potential for the next 5 years or so (i.e., between now and FY 2029). As an example, the EPS growth projections for PYPL paint a robust picture in the next few years. The EPS is estimated to reach $5.35 for FY 2025, growing at a YOY rate of 14.98%. This growth expectation is projected to continue in the next 4 years, with YOY growth rates in a range between 9.12% and 16.93%. All told, the average growth rate in the next 5 years is about 14%.

With such a growth projection, the stock is valued attractively both in absolute terms (with an FWD P/E ratio of only 12.57x) and also in terms of growth-adjusted metrics. With the average projected growth rate, the PEG ratio (P/E growth ratio) under the current price is only ~0.9x, noticeably below the 1x threshold most GARP investors (growth at reasonable price) seek.

I indeed see multiple drives that could support the above growth projection, judging by its Q3 ER. Business prospects appear very bright in my view for the long term. I consider the company one of the best established in the digital payment space. The company also enjoys excellent geographical exposure, with a presence in a host of countries around the globe. Additionally, the company features a multitude of innovations in its pipeline. Notable examples include PayPal World (slated to launch in the fall) and the various partnership agreements it has recently inked. Two such examples involve the partnership announced with Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) and also OpenAI since my last writing.

Finally, the company also features a strong balance sheet, which should make external acquisitions feasible to add further growth optionality. As a reflection, PayPal's leverage ratio (in terms of debt-to-EBITDA ratio) currently sits at 1.717 only, both substantially below the 5-year average of 1.851 and also near the lowest levels in this period.

Other Risks and Final Thoughts

Switching back to the near term, I also see good fundamental reasons to prolong the ongoing consolidation. My first major concern is the deceleration in core user engagement, judging by its Q3 updates. Despite the robust growth in TPV as aforementioned, the transactions per active account (TPA), a measure of user engagement, showed some concerning trends. My interpretation of the divergence between TPV and TPA is twofold: 1) that larger-volume transactions through Payment Service Provider, or PSP, channels are the main driving force behind the TPV, and 2) that the PYPL platform is struggling to deepen its relationship with the vast active accounts in the face of alternative offerings from other providers.

Secondly, I am also seeing some margin pressure in the near term. As a reflection, the chart below compares PYPL's current profit margin against its historical norms. The latest reading is 14.74%. The good news is that it's still slightly above the long-term average margin of 14.01%. But as seen, the margin has been declining in recent quarters and is noticeably below the peak of 17.46% observed at the end of 2023.

To wrap up, this examination of PYPL follow-up is promoted mostly by two catalysts since my last writing: its FQ3 ER and the technical trading indicators following the release of the ER. These changes have reduced the stock's timeliness in the near term, even though I still feel bullish about its long-term return potential. In the near term, I see large odds for PYPL to continue its current consolidation. This consideration has caused me to adjust its rating to buy from my earlier strong buy rating.

data.title: Zoom Q3 Earnings Preview: Watch For AI To Drive Additional Acceleration
data.description:
We're now in the thick of the Q3 earnings season, and for the most part, the cycle has been quite a negative one for software companies. As more and more investor attention has been drawn to the high-profile darlings of the AI world, the markets have gradually left the pandemic era's most popular software products behind in the dust.

Zoom (ZM), the ubiquitous videoconferencing application, has continued to draw market skeptics. The stock is still about ~flat for the year, underperforming a 15%+ jump in the S&P 500. The question for investors now is: will Q3 provide the upside catalyst Zoom needs, or will it continue to flatline?

I last wrote a strong buy article on Zoom in August, when the stock was trading near $73 per share. Since then, shares of Zoom have recovered about ~10%, though I'm holding out for bigger gains. I'm continuing to hold onto Zoom as one of the true long-term plays in my portfolio, as I'm convinced that its highly horizontal software product is part of the critical fabric of the modern workspace, and I'm reiterating my strong buy rating here (note that I rarely have this much conviction in individual names.

It's important to note that while Zoom is no longer the growth company that it was during the pandemic, the company is (gradually) achieving stabilization in its core business and re-accelerating growth.

As shown in the chart above, taken from the company's recent investor presentation at its annual Zoomtopia conference, the company is planning for slight growth acceleration this year, which is driven primarily by adding monetization to its Zoom AI Copilot for enterprise clients as well as a recent improving trend for churn rates in online (smaller segment) customers.

In my view, Zoom's Q3 release will show progress against the company's resurgence, and read-through from software companies that have already reported should support that narrative. Buy here before earnings and stay long.

Q3 Preview: Core Metrics To Track, And What Items To Watch Out For

The next major catalyst for Zoom is its Q3 earnings release, which is set for Monday, November 24. To understand where Zoom is going, let's first quickly refresh on where Zoom has been. The chart below showcases Zoom's core trended metrics up through Q2:

In Q2, Zoom's revenue accelerated to 4.7% y/y growth, up 180bps sequentially from 2.9% growth in Q1. For Q3, Zoom has guided to $1.210-$1.215 billion in revenue, which reflects 2.8-3.2% y/y growth; while Wall Street consensus is at the 3.0% growth midpoint of that range).

There is reason to believe, from Zoom's own historical guidance patterns, that the company may be positioned for a beat-and-raise. In Q2, the company had guided to $1.195-$1.200 billion (2.8-3.3% y/y growth), and ended up delivering 140bps of growth over the high end of its range. ~3% growth appears to be a "comfortable" guidance range for Zoom, and yet the company recently has tended to deliver higher.

Other key metrics to watch out for other than headline revenue are the company's RPO growth and online churn rates. To me, healthy RPO expansion signals Zoom's ability to sign longer-term and larger contracts with enterprise clients, which would help stabilize its revenue base for the long haul. We're also focused on the company's online churn rates, as this segment currently represents just shy of 40% of overall revenue.

We also think there will be growing attention on Zoom's ability to leverage AI to cut its own cost base. The company last conducted a round of layoffs impacting "only" 2% of its workforce in February 2023. We're hopeful that the company can begin to leverage automation to simplify its organization and drive meaningful profit growth, since its top-line growth is more muted.

Positive Read-Through From Other Software Companies

Despite the sharp negative reaction that many software companies have gotten in the Q3 earnings season, I think there are several notable positive data points from software companies that have already reported during the Q3 earnings season - which should help to justify positive trends on the metrics noted above.

  • Microsoft (MSFT): The software giant reported results in late October, and what we're attuned to here is the company's commercial cloud segment, which houses Microsoft Teams (a direct competitor to Zoom). Commercial cloud revenue grew 15% y/y in constant currency in the quarter, not decelerating relative to 15% growth in Q2. I take this as a positive read-through for enterprise retention rates for Zoom, though we do note that Teams could be an increasing competitive threat if Microsoft's bundling resonates with more customers.
  • HubSpot (HUBS): HubSpot, the inbound marketing software company, is a great proxy for Zoom's Online business because the company is geared primarily to small and mid-sized clients. HubSpot reported 21% y/y revenue growth that actually accelerated three points y/y, and billings were also strong at 18% y/y, despite the stock's plunge since Q2. With Zoom specifically, there is plenty of worry that the company's exposure to its frailer Online segment may weaken Zoom amid a tougher macroeconomy: and yet, HubSpot's ability to hold onto its revenue growth rates suggests that the SMB space isn't exactly blowing up.
  • Atlassian (TEAM): To a large extent, part of the worry surrounding Zoom is the fact that AI may drive reductions in headcount, which reduce paid seats. Atlassian has also been the brunt of similar worries, particularly since the company sells primarily to software developers who are directly at risk of being replaced by vibe coding and AI. In fact, Atlassian said in its earnings update that developers using vibe coding tools like Replit are more likely to expand seats and cross-sell to other products. Its ~21% y/y revenue growth rate held up versus the prior quarter, helped by improving monetization of its Rovo AI: which is also a positive signal for heightened monetization of Zoom Copilot.

That said, I won't cast the illusion that the entire quarter has been rosy so far for the software sector (as evidenced by sharp declines in many names). We note that Dropbox, which is another proxy for Zoom's online business, reported sequential declines in ARR as well as paying users. We do think, however, that Dropbox is particularly challenged due to competition from Google Drive (which is free) and Box (BOX), which is better-equipped for enterprise: so these same problems may not necessarily slip over to Zoom.

Valuation And Key Takeaways

Above all, however, investors should remain comfortable investing in Zoom for its sheer cheapness. At current share prices near $84, the stock trades at a market cap of just $25.15 billion (note that Zoom topped $100 billion in market value during the pandemic). After we net off the sizable $7.79 billion of cash on Zoom's latest balance sheet, which is free of debt, Zoom's enterprise value is just $17.36 billion.

For next year, FY27 (the year for Zoom ending in January 2027), Wall Street is expecting $5.00 billion in revenue (+3% y/y) for the company, and $5.92 in pro forma EPS. This positions Zoom's valuation multiples at 3.5x EV/FY27 revenue, and 14.2x P/E (or just 9.8x P/E on an ex-cash basis). Needless to say, buying a <10x P/E software company with recurring revenue in today's market is rare.

Stay long here: in my view, Zoom will continue to demonstrate resilience post-Q3.

data.title: Diageo: Leadership Change, Accelerating Self-Help Measures, Still A Buy
data.description:
In light of Diageo’s (DEO) (OTCPK:DGEAF) Q3 results and the CEO transition, we should brief readers on the latest development. The company does not need an introduction: it is the world's largest premium spirits player, with an estimated market share of approximately 30%. We have a long-standing buy rating; however, despite buying the dips, we are in negative territory. YTD, Diageo shares are down by almost 27% (and -9% since our last update - Fig. 1).

Despite cautious tape and sluggish spirits, we remain Buyers - "Controlling The Controllable." The latest quarter delivered steady top-line performance, robust free cash generation, and share gains in priority markets. Management is also stepping up efficiency — lifting the savings target to $625 million — while tighter CAPEX (with lower D&A) supports a clear path to margin expansion.

Fig 1

Q1 Results and Our Positive Take

Diageo delivered top-line sales growth of 2.2% to $4.9 billion (Fig. 2). This was due to disposals, negative price MIX, and slightly negative FX. On a geographical basis, sales were weak in APAC, particularly in Chinese spirits. Also, the US did not perform well; however, the EU, LATAM, and Africa almost offset the weaker impact on spirits results in Asia and North America. There is limited financial disclosure in Diageo's Q3 release; however, the company provided key messages on its accelerate programme that confirmed cost savings of a total estimate of $625 million over the next three years. Additionally, Diageo updated its organic sales and EBIT outlook, remaining committed to delivering $3 billion in FCF and maintaining a debt ratio between 2.5x and 3.0x.

Source: Diageo Q1 2026 results ppt - Fig 2

At first glance, this might seem like no news; however, we conducted a deep dive with the new CEO after the earnings call to provide first-hand insights into Diageo's investment case.

Starting with the CEO, Dave Lewis, the new Diageo leader who is a seasoned operator with deep brand-building and strong retailer insight. He has a track record of turnarounds with a high-performance mindset. He will have tight cost control, with a sharp focus on absolute profit and cash. Therefore, we see a positive intake. Additionally, the current management team has spent the past four months scoping the forward plan. The new CEO will imprint his own direction, but he confirmed the company's target. The CFO reported that spending is not underweight, but efficiency will rise. There are internal tools that were not consistently applied, so the plan is to tighten control and lean more on data & analytics.

We still see continued softness in the USA and China. Tailwinds are expected from Guinness momentum, with additional capacity coming online and greater emphasis on accessible price points in Scotch and Tequila. Diageo expects a slowdown with no improvement ahead in Q2. Management views category weakness as cyclical, while the shift to smaller formats/sizes could be more structural. Premiumization is not over, but it is in a cycle, with growth still likely ahead. This is supported by distributors who are trimming small brands and slow movers. This will favor the most prominent players, such as Diageo.

There is no fundamental change in Diageo's disposal strategy. Guinness and the Moët Hennessy stake are not for sale, though management will always explore value-unlock options. The RCB cricket franchise is non-core and not necessary to run a leading spirits business; there is significant market interest.

Earnings Change And Valuation

Q2 organic sales are expected to decrease, driving an overall H1 sales decline. US sales are likely to deteriorate further, dragged down by Tequila; however, there are two positives: 1) Diageo's leadership team flagged early benefits in business model changes in the EU, with strong spirits sales momentum in Turkey. Diageo's new products are closer to consumers, enabling more consumption occasions, and 2) the company's outlook assumes current trends persist. Still, we believe H2 pickup is expected thanks to better comps and the Chinese New Year's Eve.

In our last assessment, we forecasted no top-line growth in 2026 with a slight improvement in the EBIT. We now see Q2 organic sales at -3.5%, driving H1 sales to -1.5%. Continuing to remain positive on Diageo's H2 results, we now lower organic sales estimates by 0.5%, while maintaining an EBIT of +2.1% supported by the Accelerate cost savings of at least 75 basis points. So, our sales and EBIT are now $20 billion and $4.7 billion, respectively. There is no change in CAPEX, interest expenses ($880 million), and corporate tax (25%) (Fig. 3). Assuming no negative/positive one-off, our net profit estimate reached $3.50 billion. This implies an EPS of $6.29 in ADR and £1.57. With no buyback, Diageo has an outstanding share of 555.58 million American Depositary Receipts and 2.23 billion for DGE: LSE. Therefore, with an unchanged P/E target of 18.5x, our valuation reached £29 ($151.8 in ADR). Our valuation is aligned with the company's historical average.

Fig 3

Risks

Diageo's downside risks include a continuous slowdown in spirits consumption, which is also subject to weather conditions and consumer variance. Additionally, we report lower-than-anticipated savings costs. Diageo is also exposed to changes in COGS, FX volatility, and energy prices. Constraints in marketing, higher taxes on alcohol products, and regulatory changes. We also see pressure on lower valuation multiples from Wall Street analysts.

Conclusion

Despite a challenging environment, Diageo continues to drive its self-help measures. The new CEO has reaffirmed Diageo's target and FCF outlook of $3 billion. While the rebound was postponed in H2, we continue to see Diageo’s strong broad-based portfolio as an attractive asset. Our buy is confirmed.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
